findings.title: No ffmpeg timeout — hung process blocks segment indefinitely
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the ffmpeg Promise in a race against a per-segment timeout (e.g. 60 s via setTimeout + cmd.kill('SIGTERM')). On timeout, kill the command, unlink the .part file, and reject with a descriptive error. The timeout should be set to at least (segmentSeconds * 5) to allow for slow disks, but capped at 60 s. This prevents a single corrupted audio file from permanently poisoning the inFlightSegments entry and hanging all concurrent callers for that segment key.

findings.title: statSync on sourcePath in segment route throws 500 instead of 404 on ENOENT
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `const stat = statSync(sourcePath)` with a try/catch that maps ENOENT/EACCES to a 404 createError. Currently, if the file disappears between `resolveFilePath` (which calls `access()`) and `statSync` (called 4 lines later), the unhandled statSync error propagates as a 500. Pattern: `let stat; try { stat = statSync(sourcePath); } catch (err: any) { if (err.code === 'ENOENT' || err.code === 'EACCES') throw createError(404, 'NOT_FOUND', 'Track file is not readable'); throw err; }`

findings.title: serveFile calls statSync without try/catch — segment file deleted between ensureSegment and serve causes 500
findings.suggested_fix: In `serveFile`, wrap `statSync(filePath)` in a try/catch and map ENOENT to a caller-visible error (e.g. throw with code 'FILE_GONE') so the segment route can return 500 with a meaningful log rather than crashing the error handler with an untagged ENOENT. Alternatively, call `statSync` inside the route handler where createError is available, then pass `{ contentLength: stat.size }` to serveFile to avoid the double stat.

findings.title: Synchronous FS operations in hourly setInterval can block event loop on large caches
findings.suggested_fix: Replace all sync FS calls inside `startHlsCacheCleanup` (`readdirSync`, `statSync`, `unlinkSync`, `rmdirSync`) with their async equivalents (`fs/promises` versions). Use `await fs.readdir(...)`, `await fs.stat(...)`, etc., within an async IIFE inside the `setInterval` callback. This prevents the cleanup from blocking the Node.js event loop on caches with hundreds of track directories, which would delay all concurrent segment requests during that tick.

findings.title: Cache cleanup race: active ffmpeg write can race with rmdirSync deleting the parent directory
findings.suggested_fix: The hourly cleanup uses `stat.mtimeMs` of the directory to decide staleness. However, writing a new segment file updates the parent directory mtime, so a track being actively played will have a fresh mtime and won't be cleaned. The race is narrow: cleanup reads mtime (stale), decides to delete; concurrently, ensureSegment passes existsSync (miss), starts ffmpeg writing to .part inside the same directory; cleanup deletes the directory before ffmpeg renames. To close this: before rmdirSync, re-check that no in-flight segment key starts with the directory's trackKey prefix (`for (const key of inFlightSegments.keys()) { if (key.startsWith(entry)) skip; }`). Export `inFlightSegments` (or a read-only view) for this check.

findings.title: initHlsCacheDir does not distinguish directories from files — non-directory entries cause silent readdirSync failure
findings.suggested_fix: Before calling `readdirSync(dir)` inside `initHlsCacheDir`, check `statSync(dir).isDirectory()` (in a try/catch). Without this check, if a stray file (not a directory) exists directly under `HLS_CACHE_DIR`, the inner `readdirSync(dir)` throws ENOTDIR — currently swallowed silently. Adding an isDirectory guard mirrors the pattern already used in `startHlsCacheCleanup`.

findings.title: Lidarr root path cache is never invalidated — stale path survives Lidarr root folder reconfiguration
findings.suggested_fix: The `lidarrRootPath` / `lidarrRootPromise` cache has no TTL. If a Lidarr admin changes the root folder path while the server is running, all subsequent track file resolutions silently remap to the wrong path, resulting in ENOENT 404s until restart. Consider adding a TTL of ~5 minutes or adding a cache-bust endpoint (`/api/admin/refresh-lidarr-cache`). This is pre-existing but becomes more impactful with HLS since a misresolved path causes the entire segment generation to fail rather than just one streaming response.

findings.title: Rejected shared promise from ensureSegment propagates correctly but leaves failed callers with generic 500
findings.suggested_fix: When generateSegment rejects, all concurrent waiters sharing the same inFlightSegments promise receive the raw ffmpeg Error (e.g. 'ffmpeg exited with code 1'). This flows to next(err) in the route handler and becomes a 500 with code INTERNAL_ERROR. The fix from finding #1 (timeout + kill) partially addresses this. Additionally, consider wrapping the generateSegment rejection in the ensureSegment catch block to attach a structured createError(500, 'TRANSCODE_FAILED', ...) so logs and client responses carry a typed code rather than a raw ffmpeg message string.

findings.title: startHlsCacheCleanup timer is not unref'd — prevents clean process exit if shutdown races the interval
findings.suggested_fix: Call `.unref()` on the return value of `setInterval(...)` in `startHlsCacheCleanup`. This matches the pattern used in `index.ts` for `forceKillTimer.unref()` and means the cleanup interval won't prevent Node from exiting when `server.close()` is called during graceful shutdown (hourly cleanup fires are acceptable to lose on shutdown). Without `.unref()`, if the server receives SIGTERM exactly when the cleanup interval fires, the cleanup's synchronous FS operations may block shutdown acknowledgement.

findings.title: buildPlaylist does not validate startSegment < segmentCount at the service level
findings.suggested_fix: The validation `if (startSegment >= segmentCount)` is in the route handler (stream.ts line 74) but not in `buildPlaylist` itself. If buildPlaylist is called with startSegment >= segmentCount (e.g. from future callers or tests), the for-loop emits no segments and produces a playlist with only headers + EXT-X-ENDLIST, which is technically valid HLS but will cause the player to immediately signal end-of-track. Add a guard or assertion inside buildPlaylist, or document the precondition clearly.

residual_risks: Corrupted audio files (e.g. truncated FLAC) will cause ffmpeg to produce a zero-byte or malformed .part file; after rename it will be served as a 200 audio/mpeg with empty or garbage bytes. The HLS client may loop retrying or stall silently. There is no post-transcode validation of segment size or MP3 frame integrity.

The inFlightSegments Map is process-local. In a multi-process deployment (PM2 cluster mode), two worker processes can race to generate the same segment simultaneously, each writing to the same .part path. The last writer wins (both rename over each other), which is safe for correctness but wastes CPU. A file-lock or advisory lock on the .part path would prevent duplicate work.

HLS_CACHE_DIR is cleaned hourly but segments for tracks currently being played by any user can be evicted if the directory mtime is stale (e.g. a 4-hour-long track where early segments weren't accessed for 25 h). Eviction during playback forces re-transcoding on the next segment request; the player will stall briefly. The mtime-based eviction strategy does not account for ongoing playback sessions.

The segment route resolves the source file path from Lidarr on every segment request (no per-request caching within a session). For a 40-segment track, this means 40 round-trips to Lidarr (getTrack + getTrackFile + rootfolder). Under concurrent playback this multiplies. A short TTL cache (30 s) keyed on trackId would reduce Lidarr load significantly.

On Windows, renameSync across volumes (e.g. HLS_CACHE_DIR on D:, source on C:) is not atomic and may fail with EXDEV. The .part file would remain, and the next request would retry ffmpeg which would overwrite it — eventually succeeding if the rename issue is transient, but looping if the cache dir is always on a different volume.
testing_gaps: No test covers the ffmpeg timeout scenario (finding #1). A test that makes the mock ffmpeg never fire 'end' or 'error' events should verify the segment request eventually fails with a timeout rather than hanging indefinitely.

No test covers concurrent callers sharing a rejected promise from ensureSegment — the unit test covers deduplication on success but not on failure. Need a test that fires two concurrent ensureSegment calls, makes ffmpeg error, and asserts both callers receive the rejection.

No integration test covers the statSync failure between resolveFilePath and statSync in the segment route (finding #2). A test that makes the fixture file disappear mid-request (after the Lidarr stubs respond but before statSync) is hard but the unit path can be tested by mocking statSync to throw ENOENT after fs.access resolves.

The hourly cleanup is not tested at all (startHlsCacheCleanup). No test verifies that directories older than 24 h are removed, or that active directories are preserved, or that the cleanup does not race with in-flight generation.
No test covers the startSegment playlist endpoint validation edge case where startSegment equals segmentCount (off-by-one boundary).

No test covers a Lidarr 404 during segment generation (i.e. track deleted from Lidarr between playlist fetch and segment fetch) — the integration test suite only covers happy-path Lidarr responses for the segment route.
